meta.name: Starter English Deck
meta.note: Add your own words here. Each entry: word, pos, definition, example, distractors (3 plausible-but-wrong meanings used by the quiz game).

words.definition: present, appearing, or found everywhere
words.example: Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern life.
distractors: extremely rare or hard to find

words.definition: lasting for a very short time
words.example: Fame can be ephemeral, vanishing as fast as it arrives.

words.definition: dealing with things sensibly and realistically
words.example: She took a pragmatic approach and fixed the urgent bug first.

words.definition: truthful and straightforward; frank
words.example: He gave a candid review of the plan's weak points.

words.definition: showing great attention to detail; very careful
words.example: The accountant was meticulous about every figure.

words.definition: able to recover quickly from difficulties
words.example: The resilient team bounced back after the setback.

words.definition: open to more than one interpretation; unclear
words.example: The instructions were ambiguous, so nobody knew what to do.

words.definition: showing careful and persistent effort
words.example: A diligent student, she reviewed her notes every night.

words.definition: based on random choice rather than reason
words.example: The deadline felt arbitrary, with no logic behind it.

words.definition: giving a lot of information clearly in a few words
words.example: Keep the summary concise—three sentences at most.

words.definition: holding firmly to a purpose; persistent
words.example: Her tenacious effort finally paid off.

words.definition: seeming reasonable or probable
words.example: It's a plausible explanation, but we need proof.

words.definition: unwilling and hesitant
words.example: He was reluctant to share his password.

words.definition: using more words than needed
words.example: The report was verbose and hard to skim.

words.definition: featuring new methods or ideas
words.example: Their innovative design won the award.

words.definition: to examine closely and critically
words.example: Auditors scrutinize every transaction.

words.definition: to make less severe or serious
words.example: Backups mitigate the risk of data loss.

words.definition: to publicly support or recommend
words.example: She advocates for cleaner energy.

words.definition: to express an idea clearly
words.example: He articulated the plan in one sentence.

words.definition: to assign a task to another person
words.example: Good managers delegate rather than micromanage.

words.definition: to combine into a single, stronger whole
words.example: We consolidated three reports into one.

words.definition: to make suffering or a problem less intense
words.example: The medicine alleviated her headache.

words.definition: to draw out a response or reaction
words.example: The question elicited nervous laughter.

words.definition: to weaken gradually or secretly
words.example: Constant criticism can undermine confidence.

words.definition: to make consistent or compatible; to restore friendship
words.example: They reconciled the two conflicting reports.

words.definition: acting with care and thought for the future
words.example: It's prudent to save part of every paycheck.

words.definition: logical and consistent; easy to follow
words.example: She gave a coherent argument that convinced everyone.

words.definition: not needed because it is more than enough or repeated
words.example: The extra step was redundant, so we removed it.

words.definition: strong and healthy; able to withstand stress
words.example: The system is robust enough to handle heavy traffic.

words.definition: of little value or importance
words.example: Don't waste time on trivial details.

words.definition: very great or intense; showing deep insight
words.example: The book had a profound effect on her.

words.definition: no longer produced or used; out of date
words.example: Floppy disks are now obsolete.

words.definition: very complicated or detailed
words.example: The watch has an intricate mechanism.

words.definition: possible to do easily or conveniently
words.example: Finishing by Friday is feasible if we start now.

words.definition: famous for something bad
words.example: The area is notorious for traffic jams.

words.definition: very skilled or proficient
words.example: She's adept at solving tricky problems.

words.definition: the quality of being open and honest
words.example: I appreciated his candor about the risks.

words.definition: general agreement among a group
words.example: The team reached a consensus after debate.

words.definition: a typical example or model of something
words.example: The discovery created a new scientific paradigm.

words.definition: the art of effective or persuasive speaking or writing
words.example: His speech was full of powerful rhetoric.

words.definition: a subtle difference in meaning or tone
words.example: She caught every nuance in his reply.

words.definition: the freedom to decide, or care in keeping secrets
words.example: Use your discretion when replying to clients.

words.definition: a natural ability or talent
words.example: He has a real aptitude for languages.

words.definition: careful and persistent work or effort
words.example: Her diligence earned the team's trust.

words.definition: to make something difficult; to hold back
words.example: Poor lighting can hinder progress.

words.definition: to declare public approval or support
words.example: The mayor endorsed the new policy.

words.definition: to make a process simpler and more efficient
words.example: We streamlined the checkout to two clicks.

words.definition: to gather and put together into one collection
words.example: She compiled the data into a single sheet.

words.definition: to expect or predict; to prepare for
words.example: Good drivers anticipate hazards early.

words.definition: to become progressively worse
words.example: The weather deteriorated by evening.
